summary:
The dominant driver across all categories this morning is the ongoing US-Israel military conflict with Iran and its cascading effects on the global economy, energy markets, and UK security posture. The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed to commercial shipping since late February, cutting roughly 20% of global daily oil supply and sending Brent crude above $107/bbl. The OECD has cut its UK GDP growth forecast for 2026 to 0.7% (from 1.2%) while raising the UK inflation forecast to 4%, with some economists warning of 4.6–5% by Q4. The UK is now directly exposed militarily after Iranian drone strikes on RAF Akrotiri in Cyprus in early March, and remains engaged in around-the-clock defensive air operations there.

Domestically, a six-day BMA resident doctors strike is confirmed for 7–13 April, adding NHS pressure. The political landscape is fragmented heading into local, Senedd, and Scottish Parliament elections on 7 May 2026, with Reform UK consistently leading in polls and Labour under sustained pressure. Cyber threats to UK CNI have intensified sharply, with 93% of CNI organisations reporting incidents in the past year. The UK weather picture has stabilised after an extremely wet winter, with no severe active weather hazard as of this update.

categories.name: Economic / Financial
categories.rationale: The Strait of Hormuz disruption since late February has caused acute commodity and energy market stress. Brent crude spiked to approximately $107–$112/bbl on 2 April, driven by tanker traffic collapsing by around 70% and major shipping firms (Maersk, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd) suspending Gulf transits. The OECD has cut UK 2026 GDP growth to 0.7% and raised its UK CPI forecast to 4%, with credible scenarios pointing to 4.6–5% by Q4 if energy prices persist. GBP/USD closed at 1.3229 on 3 April, down from a year high of 1.3867 in January, near its 2026 low. The Bank of England FPC maintained the CCyB rate at 2% in its April 2026 record, acknowledging a 'substantial macroeconomic shock' while noting the banking system remains adequately capitalised. FTSE 100 is volatile, having dipped to around 10,100–10,170 before a partial recovery to 10,337–10,436 on 1–2 April on intermittent de-escalation signals. The cost of living response from the government (NLW increase to £12.71, energy cap cut by £117) partially cushions households but cannot offset the scale of the energy price shock.
drivers: Strait of Hormuz effectively closed; tanker traffic down ~70%; LNG and crude supply disrupted
Brent crude ~$107–$112/bbl on 2 April, up ~$41 year-on-year
OECD cuts UK 2026 GDP forecast to 0.7%; raises CPI forecast to 4%
GBP/USD at 1.3229 on 3 April, near its 2026 low
FTSE 100 volatile, multiple risk-off sessions in March; partial recovery on de-escalation signals

categories.name: Military / Security
categories.rationale: The UK is now operationally engaged in the Middle East conflict in a defensive capacity. Iranian drone strikes hit RAF Akrotiri (Cyprus) on 1 March, injuring three military dependents and damaging a hangar. The UK has deployed additional Typhoons, F-35s, Wildcat helicopters, Merlin early warning aircraft, and HMS Dragon (destroyer) in support. The UK formally permitted limited US use of British bases for strikes on Iranian missile and launch sites. The IRGC has explicitly designated RAF Akrotiri as a target. Simultaneously, the Ukraine-Russia conflict continues at high intensity: Russia claims full control of Luhansk, Ukraine launched a mass drone attack on Russian energy infrastructure, and Russia has conducted waves of 700+ drones in 24-hour periods. NATO has reinforced the eastern flank under Baltic Sentry and Eastern Sentry operations. UK national terrorism threat level remains at SUBSTANTIAL, with intelligence assessments noting expanded Iranian transnational repression activity on UK soil, including disrupted assassination plots. UK government has refused military base use for direct strikes on Iranian political/economic targets.
drivers: RAF Akrotiri struck by Iranian drone (1 March); UK now in active defensive operations over Cyprus
IRGC has designated Akrotiri a target; further attacks assessed as credible
UK terrorism threat level: SUBSTANTIAL; Iranian transnational repression activity in UK increasing
Ukraine-Russia conflict at sustained high intensity; Russia claims Luhansk; mass drone exchanges continue
NATO eastern flank reinforced; European defence spending rising but capability gaps remain

categories.name: Political / Governance
categories.rationale: The UK political environment is under significant stress. Polling consistently shows Reform UK leading Labour nationally, and major local, Scottish Parliament, and Senedd elections are scheduled for 7 May 2026. There is speculation about a potential Labour leadership challenge if May results are poor. The Starmer government has faced repeated internal rebellions (immigration, jury trial reforms, welfare), and the COBR(M) format has been activated in response to the Middle East crisis and domestic cost-of-living pressures. A 6-day NHS resident doctors strike (BMA) is confirmed for 7–13 April after talks collapsed, representing the 15th walkout in this dispute. Institutions are functioning but under sustained pressure. There is no constitutional crisis or breakdown of governance.
drivers: May 2026 elections (local, Senedd, Scottish Parliament) approaching; Reform UK leading national polls
COBR(M) activated for Middle East crisis and cost-of-living response
BMA resident doctors 6-day strike confirmed 7–13 April; talks collapsed
Repeated Labour internal rebellions on immigration, court reform, and welfare
Potential Labour leadership speculation if May local election results are poor

categories.name: Natural Hazards
categories.rationale: The UK experienced its wettest winter on record through early 2026, including Somerset declaring a Major Incident and multiple named storms (Goretti, Ingrid, Chandra). As of late February–March, high pressure has been building and a seasonal turnaround is underway. The Environment Agency had five flood warnings and eight alerts active in late February, primarily in Somerset, Devon, and Dorset, but these have been reducing. April is expected to be drier and milder, around 1.5°C above the seasonal average. No active severe weather warnings or major hazard events are current as of 3 April. The Middle East conflict has not generated direct natural hazard impacts on UK territory.
drivers: Wettest winter on record; Somerset Major Incident now downgraded
Flood warnings reducing through late March; no active severe weather warnings as of 3 April
Spring outlook drier and milder; Azores High building; active hazard risk temporarily reduced

categories.name: Social Stability
categories.rationale: Social tension is elevated on several fronts. The BMA resident doctors strike (7–13 April) is the 15th such walkout since 2023 and will significantly disrupt planned NHS care. A large anti-far-right march in London on 28 March drew an estimated 50,000 (Met Police) to 500,000 (organisers), reflecting deep political polarisation around the rise of Reform UK. An 'Everyone Day' protest at Trafalgar Square is planned for 11 April. Immigration policy, cost-of-living pressures, and Middle East conflict all feed civil friction. Cost-of-living measures from 1 April (NLW increase, energy cap cut) provide some relief but the gap between household stress and available support remains significant given the energy price shock.
drivers: BMA resident doctors strike confirmed 7–13 April; 15th walkout in this dispute
Major anti-far-right protest in London (28 March); significant political polarisation evident
Energy price shock and cost-of-living pressures increasing household stress
Further protest events planned for April; immigration policy a continued flashpoint

categories.name: Critical Infrastructure / Cyber
categories.rationale: Cyber risk to UK CNI is at an elevated baseline and trending upward. Bridewell's CNI Report 2026 (March) found 93% of UK CNI organisations had suffered a cyber incident in the past year. The NCSC's 2025 Annual Review recorded 204 nationally significant incidents in the year to August 2025, up from 89 the previous year, with 18 classified as highly significant. In March 2026, the NCSC confirmed AI-powered attacks have surged, with the UK experiencing a 38% year-on-year increase in reported cyber incidents and AI implicated in ~60% of sophisticated attacks. Russian-aligned hacktivist groups have continued DDoS and disruption attacks targeting UK organisations; the NCSC issued a specific warning in January 2026. The government's Cyber Security and Resilience Bill is in progress but not yet enacted. The government's Spring Statement (3 March) omitted direct cyber investment commitments, attracting criticism from the ISF. There are no known major CNI outages active at this update.
drivers: 93% of UK CNI organisations reported a cyber incident in past year (Bridewell CNI Report 2026)
NCSC handled 204 significant incidents to August 2025, up from 89 the prior year
AI-powered attacks up 38% YoY; AI implicated in ~60% of sophisticated UK incidents
Russian-aligned hacktivist DDoS activity targeting UK organisations; NCSC warning January 2026
Cyber Security and Resilience Bill in progress; Spring Statement omitted direct cyber investment

indicators.name: Brent crude
indicators.note: Spiked to ~$111.69 on 2 April amid Strait of Hormuz disruption; approx. $41 above year-ago level. WTI briefly flipped above Brent due to supply rerouting.

indicators.name: GBP/USD
indicators.note: Closed 3 April at 1.3229, near its 2026 low of 1.3160 (31 March). Down from 1.3867 high in January. Pound under pressure from Middle East shock and weak growth outlook.

indicators.name: FTSE 100
indicators.note: Partial recovery 1–2 April (up ~1.6% and ~0.7%) after multiple risk-off sessions in March. Month-on-month still down ~5.3%; year-on-year up ~18%.

indicators.name: UK CPI (OECD forecast)
indicators.note: OECD raised UK 2026 CPI forecast from 2.5% to 4.0%. Some economists cite 4.6–5% as credible if energy prices persist at current levels into summer.

indicators.name: Strait of Hormuz traffic
indicators.note: Major container and tanker operators have suspended transits. Iran has made 21+ confirmed attacks on merchant ships as of mid-March. UK-led 40-nation coalition convened 2 April to explore diplomatic options.

indicators.name: UK terrorism threat
indicators.note: JTAC/MI5 classification unchanged. Iranian transnational repression and proxy activity assessed as increased on UK soil. Multiple plots disrupted in recent period.

changes_since_last_update: First live evidence-based intelligence assessment replaces initialization placeholder.
Strait of Hormuz effectively closed since late February; oil at ~$107–$112/bbl on 2 April.
RAF Akrotiri struck by Iranian drone 1 March; UK now in active defensive air operations over Cyprus.
OECD cut UK 2026 GDP forecast to 0.7%; CPI forecast raised to 4%.
BMA 6-day resident doctors strike confirmed for 7–13 April.
UK anti-far-right march on 28 March; further protest planned 11 April.
NCSC confirmed 38% YoY increase in UK cyber incidents; AI implicated in ~60% of sophisticated attacks.
priority_watch_items: Strait of Hormuz: any sustained reopening or further escalation is the single highest-impact variable for UK energy prices and inflation.
BMA strike 7–13 April: monitor NHS pressure and whether derogation processes are activated.
May 7 elections (local, Senedd, Scottish Parliament): Reform UK performance will shape political stability outlook.
Oil price trajectory and Bank of England response: watch for rate signals if CPI approaches 4.5–5%.
Iranian follow-on attacks on RAF Akrotiri or UK-linked assets: assess for direct homeland security implications.
source_notes: Economic data sourced from Fortune/Reuters (Brent crude), poundsterlinglive.com (GBP/USD), Trading Economics/inkl (FTSE 100), Evening Standard/OECD (macroeconomic forecasts), Bank of England FPC Record April 2026.

Military/security data sourced from Wikipedia (2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis; 2026 drone strikes on Akrotiri), BBC News, MSB Protection UK Security Brief March 2026, Defense and Tech (Ukraine frontline April 2026), Behorizon.org strategic brief 2 April 2026.
Political data sourced from GOV.UK, Wikipedia (2026 UK politics and government; 2026 local elections), BBC News, Sky News.
Natural hazard data sourced from YouTube/Met Office spring 2026 forecast; Environment Agency flood warnings (late February 2026).
Social stability data sourced from Le Monde/Al Jazeera (Together Alliance march 28 March), NHS England and GOV.UK (BMA industrial action April 2026), Action Network (Everyone Day protest 11 April).

Cyber data sourced from Bridewell CNI Report 2026 (Smart Buildings Magazine), Cloudswitched/NCSC (AI-powered attacks March 2026), Transputec (UK cyber threats 2026), Industrial Cyber (UK government CNI cyber warning).

Confidence is high for the economic and military categories where multiple independent sources converge. The FTSE 100 value reflects 1–2 April data; today's open (3 April) was not yet captured. GBP/USD is the 3 April opening rate.
